rationale: Title X is more informative and direct: it names both technologies, specifies the key finding (comparable arrhythmia freedom), the efficiency advantage (shorter procedures), the design (propensity-matched multicenter), and the sample size (117 patients) — all within a single readable sentence. Title Y uses a vague feasibility/safety/efficacy triad that buries the comparative result and reads more like a feasibility pilot than a definitive comparison. For JICE, which values concise, result-forward titles, X's head-to-head framing is stronger.
loser_weakness: Title Y front-loads 'feasibility, safety, and efficacy' — a generic triad that obscures the study's actual comparative finding. Placing the comparison with RF as a secondary clause ('propensity-matched comparison') makes the design appear subordinate, and omitting sample size and the procedural-time advantage reduces informativeness. The phrasing 'multicenter experience' also connotes a descriptive registry rather than a controlled comparison, underselling the study design.
